title: Michael (archangel)

infobox.value: * [Judaism](/wiki/Judaism "Judaism") * All [Christian denominations](/wiki/Christian_denomination "Christian denomination") which [venerate saints](/wiki/Veneration_of_saints "Veneration of saints") * [Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam") * [Bahá'í Faith](/wiki/Bah%C3%A1%27%C3%AD_Faith "Bahá'í Faith")
[Mont Saint-Michel](/wiki/Mont_Saint-Michel "Mont Saint-Michel"), [Sanctuary of Saint Michael the Archangel](/wiki/Sanctuary_of_Saint_Michael_the_Archangel "Sanctuary of Saint Michael the Archangel")

* 29 September ("[Michaelmas](/wiki/Michaelmas "Michaelmas")" – [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church"), [Anglican Communion](/wiki/Anglican_Communion "Anglican Communion") and [Lutheranism](/wiki/Lutheranism "Lutheranism")) * [8 November](/wiki/8_November_(Orthodox_liturgics) "8 November (Orthodox liturgics)") (New Calendar [Eastern Orthodox Churches](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Churches "Eastern Orthodox Churches")) * 8 November ([Eastern Catholic Churches](/wiki/Eastern_Catholic_Churches "Eastern Catholic Churches")) * 21 November (Old Calendar Eastern Orthodox Churches) * 12th of each month in [Coptic calendar](/wiki/Coptic_calendar "Coptic calendar") (Coptic Churches) * Many other local and historical feasts
banner, [scales](/wiki/Weighing_scale "Weighing scale"), weighing souls, sword, slaying Satan or a dragon

Guardian of the [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church"),[[1]](#cite_note-Alban-1) [Vatican City](/wiki/Vatican_City "Vatican City"), [Rome](/wiki/Rome "Rome"), [Lazio](/wiki/Lazio "Lazio"), [Italy](/wiki/Italy "Italy"),[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-catorg-3) [France](/wiki/France "France"), [Germany](/wiki/Germany "Germany"), [Israel](/wiki/Israel "Israel")[[4]](#cite_note-4) [Normandy](/wiki/Normandy "Normandy"), [France](/wiki/France "France"), [Kyiv](/wiki/Kyiv "Kyiv"), [Ukraine](/wiki/Ukraine "Ukraine"), [Laoang](/wiki/Laoang "Laoang"), dying people, [poor souls](/wiki/Poor_souls "Poor souls"),[[5]](#cite_note-5) [bankers](/wiki/Bankers "Bankers"), [grocers](/wiki/Grocery_store "Grocery store"), [Police](/wiki/Police "Police"), especially [Police Officers](/wiki/Police_officer "Police officer"), [Military](/wiki/Military "Military"), especially [military personnel](/wiki/Military_personnel "Military personnel") and others, et cetera.

or **Michael the [Taxiarch](/wiki/Taxiarch "Taxiarch")**,[[6]](#cite_note-7) is an [archangel](/wiki/Archangel "Archangel") and the warrior of [God](/wiki/God_in_Abrahamic_religions "God in Abrahamic religions") in Christianity, [Judaism](/wiki/Judaism "Judaism"), and [Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam") while additionally being a [saint](/wiki/Saint "Saint") in [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity").[[3]](#cite_note-catorg-3)[[7]](#cite_note-8) The earliest surviving mentions of his name are in third- and second-century BC Jewish works, often but not always apocalyptic, where he is the chief of the [angels](/wiki/Angels "Angels") and archangels, and he is the guardian prince of Israel and is responsible for the care of the [people](/wiki/Israelites "Israelites") of [Israel](/wiki/Biblical_Israel "Biblical Israel").[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAsale202055-9)[[9]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHannah201133–54-10)[[10]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHannah201133-11)[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarnes199354-12) Christianity conserved nearly all the Jewish traditions concerning him,[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHannah201154-13) and he is mentioned explicitly in [Revelation 12:7–12](https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Revelation+12:7–12&version=nrsvue),[[13]](#cite_note-14) where he does battle with [Satan](/wiki/Satan "Satan"),[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBromiley1971156–157-15) and in the [Epistle of Jude](/wiki/Epistle_of_Jude "Epistle of Jude"), where the archangel and the devil dispute over the body of Moses.[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHannah201146-16)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Angel in Abrahamic religions

"Saint Michael" redirects here. For other uses, see [Saint Michael (disambiguation)](/wiki/Saint_Michael_(disambiguation) "Saint Michael (disambiguation)").

**Michael**,[[a]](#cite_note-6) also called **Archangel Michael** or **Michael the [Taxiarch](/wiki/Taxiarch "Taxiarch")**,[[6]](#cite_note-7) is an [archangel](/wiki/Archangel "Archangel") and the warrior of [God](/wiki/God_in_Abrahamic_religions "God in Abrahamic religions") in Christianity, [Judaism](/wiki/Judaism "Judaism"), and [Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam") while additionally being a [saint](/wiki/Saint "Saint") in [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity").[[3]](#cite_note-catorg-3)[[7]](#cite_note-8) The earliest surviving mentions of his name are in third- and second-century BC Jewish works, often but not always apocalyptic, where he is the chief of the [angels](/wiki/Angels "Angels") and archangels, and he is the guardian prince of Israel and is responsible for the care of the [people](/wiki/Israelites "Israelites") of [Israel](/wiki/Biblical_Israel "Biblical Israel").[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAsale202055-9)[[9]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHannah201133–54-10)[[10]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHannah201133-11)[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarnes199354-12) Christianity conserved nearly all the Jewish traditions concerning him,[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHannah201154-13) and he is mentioned explicitly in [Revelation 12:7–12](https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Revelation+12:7–12&version=nrsvue),[[13]](#cite_note-14) where he does battle with [Satan](/wiki/Satan "Satan"),[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBromiley1971156–157-15) and in the [Epistle of Jude](/wiki/Epistle_of_Jude "Epistle of Jude"), where the archangel and the devil dispute over the body of Moses.[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHannah201146-16)

## Old Testament and Deuterocanon

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Michael_(archangel)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Old Testament and Deuterocanon")]

The [Book of Enoch](/wiki/Book_of_Enoch "Book of Enoch")[[10]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHannah201133-11) lists Michael as one of seven archangels (the remaining names are [Uriel](/wiki/Uriel "Uriel"), [Raguel](/wiki/Raguel_(angel) "Raguel (angel)"), [Raphael](/wiki/Raphael_(archangel) "Raphael (archangel)"), [Sariel](/wiki/Sariel "Sariel"), [Gabriel](/wiki/Gabriel "Gabriel"), and [Remiel](/wiki/Remiel "Remiel")), who in the [Book of Tobit](/wiki/Book_of_Tobit "Book of Tobit") "stand ready and enter before the glory of the Lord".[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarnes199354-12) The fact that Michael is introduced implies the knowledge of him and the other named angels.[[10]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHannah201133-11)[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECollins1999339-17) He is mentioned again in the [last chapters of the Book of Daniel](/wiki/Daniel%27s_final_vision "Daniel's final vision"), a Jewish [apocalypse](/wiki/Apocalypse "Apocalypse") composed in the second century BC, in which a man clothed in [linen](/wiki/Linen "Linen") (never identified, but matching a description given to John in [Revelation](/wiki/Book_of_Revelation "Book of Revelation") regarding the [Alpha and Omega](/wiki/Alpha_and_Omega "Alpha and Omega")) tells Daniel that he and "Michael, your prince" are engaged in a battle with the "[prince of Persia](/wiki/Territorial_spirit#Daniel_10 "Territorial spirit")", after which, at the end-time, "Michael, the great prince who protects your people, will arise".[[17]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECollins198434–36-18)[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHill2008176-19)

Enoch was instrumental in establishing the pre-eminent place of Michael among the angels and archangels, and in later Jewish works, he is said to be their chief, mediating the [Torah](/wiki/Torah "Torah") (the Law of God), and standing at the right hand of the [throne of God](/wiki/Throne_of_God "Throne of God").[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAsale202055-9) In the traditions of the [Qumran](/wiki/Qumran "Qumran") community, he defends or leads the people of God in the eschatological (i.e., end-time) battle.[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFahlbusch1999674-20) In other writings, he is responsible for the care of Israel and acts as commander of the [heavenly armies](/wiki/Heavenly_host "Heavenly host"); he is Israel's advocate contesting Satan's claim to the body of [Moses](/wiki/Moses "Moses"); he intercedes between God and humanity and serves as [High Priest](/wiki/High_Priest_of_Israel "High Priest of Israel") in the heavenly sanctuary; and he [accompanies the souls](/wiki/Psychopomp "Psychopomp") of the righteous dead to Paradise.[[9]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHannah201133–54-10)

## New Testament

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Michael_(archangel)&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: New Testament")]

The seven archangels (or four, as traditions differ but always include Michael) were associated with the branches of the [menorah](/wiki/Temple_menorah "Temple menorah"), the sacred seven-branched lampstand in the [Temple](/wiki/Temple_in_Jerusalem "Temple in Jerusalem") as the seven spirits before the throne of God, and this is reflected in the [Book of Revelation](/wiki/Book_of_Revelation "Book of Revelation") [4:5](https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Revelation+4:5&version=nrsvue) (“Coming from the throne are flashes of lightning and rumblings and peals of thunder, and in front of the throne burn seven flaming torches, which are the seven spirits of God,” – [NRSVue](/wiki/NRSVue "NRSVue")).[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarker2006123-21) Michael is mentioned explicitly in [Revelation 12](/wiki/Revelation_12 "Revelation 12"):7–12, where he does battle with Satan and casts him out of heaven so that he no longer has access to God as accuser (his formal role in the Old Testament).[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBromiley1971156–157-15) The fall of Satan at the coming of [Jesus](/wiki/Jesus "Jesus") marks the separation of the New Testament from Judaism.[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBromiley1971157-22) In [Luke](/wiki/Gospel_of_Luke "Gospel of Luke") [22:31](https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Luke+22:31&version=nrsvue), Jesus tells [Peter](/wiki/Saint_Peter "Saint Peter") that Satan has asked God for permission to “sift” the disciples, the goal being to accuse them, but the accusation is opposed by Jesus, who thus takes on the role played by angels, and especially by Michael, in Judaism.[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBromiley1971156-23)

Michael is mentioned by name for the second time in the [Epistle of Jude](/wiki/Epistle_of_Jude "Epistle of Jude"), which is an impassioned plea for the believers to engage in battle against the incursion of the error.[[23]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGreen200818-24) In verses 9–10, the author denounces the heretics by contrasting them with the archangel Michael, who, in disputing with Satan over the body of Moses, “did not presume to pronounce the verdict of 'slander' but said, 'The Lᴏʀᴅ rebuke you!'”[[24]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGreen200879-25)

## Judaism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Michael_(archangel)&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Judaism")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e6/Michael.svg/250px-Michael.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Michael.svg)

The name Michael in [Hebrew](/wiki/Hebrew_language "Hebrew language")

According to [rabbinic tradition](/wiki/Rabbinic_literature "Rabbinic literature"), Michael acted as the advocate of Israel, and sometimes had to fight with the princes of the other nations ([Daniel 10:13](https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Daniel+10:13&version=nrsvue)) and particularly with the angel [Samael](/wiki/Samael "Samael"), Israel's accuser. Their enmity dates from the time Samael was thrown from heaven and tried to drag Michael down with him, necessitating God's intervention.[[25]](#cite_note-26)[[26]](#cite_note-27)

The idea that Michael was the advocate of the Jews became so prevalent that in spite of the rabbinical prohibition against appealing to angels as intermediaries between God and his people, he held a place in the Jewish liturgy: "When a man is in need he must pray directly to God, and neither to Michael nor to [Gabriel](/wiki/Gabriel "Gabriel")."[[27]](#cite_note-28) [Jeremiah](/wiki/Jeremiah "Jeremiah") addresses a prayer to him.[[28]](#cite_note-29)

The [rabbis](/wiki/Rabbi "Rabbi") declare that Michael entered into his role of defender at the time of the biblical patriarchs. Rabbi Eliezer ben Jacob said he rescued [Abraham](/wiki/Abraham "Abraham") from the furnace into which he had been thrown by [Nimrod](/wiki/Nimrod "Nimrod") (Midrash Genesis Rabbah xliv. 16). Some say he was the "one that had escaped" ([Genesis 14:13](https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Genesis+14:13&version=nrsvue)), who told Abraham that Lot had been taken captive (Midrash Pirke R. El.), and who protected Sarah from defilement by Abimelech.

Michael prevented [Isaac](/wiki/Isaac "Isaac") from sacrifice by his father by substituting a ram in his place. He saved [Jacob](/wiki/Jacob "Jacob"), while yet in his mother's womb, from death by Samael.[[29]](#cite_note-30) He later prevented Laban from harming Jacob.(*[Pirke De-Rabbi Eliezer](/wiki/Pirke_De-Rabbi_Eliezer "Pirke De-Rabbi Eliezer")*, xxxvi).

The [midrash](/wiki/Midrash "Midrash") *[Exodus Rabbah](/wiki/Exodus_Rabbah "Exodus Rabbah")* holds that Michael exercised his function of advocate of Israel at the time of [the Exodus](/wiki/The_Exodus "The Exodus") and destroyed [Sennacherib](/wiki/Sennacherib "Sennacherib")'s army.[[30]](#cite_note-31)

## Gnosticism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Michael_(archangel)&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Gnosticism")]

In the [Secret Book of John](/wiki/Apocryphon_of_John "Apocryphon of John"), a second-century text found in the [Nag Hammadi codices](/wiki/Nag_Hammadi_library "Nag Hammadi library") of [Gnosticism](/wiki/Gnosticism "Gnosticism"), Michael is placed in control of the demons who help [Yaldabaoth](/wiki/Demiurge#Yaldabaoth "Demiurge") create [Adam](/wiki/Adam "Adam"), along with six others named Uriel, Asmenedas, Saphasatoel, Aarmouriam, Richram, and Amiorps.[[31]](#cite_note-32) According to [Origen of Alexandria](/wiki/Origen "Origen") in his work [Against Celsus](/wiki/Contra_Celsum "Contra Celsum"), Michael was represented as a [lion](/wiki/Cultural_depictions_of_lions "Cultural depictions of lions") on the [Ophite Diagram](/wiki/Ophite_Diagrams "Ophite Diagrams").[[32]](#cite_note-33)

## Christianity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Michael_(archangel)&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Christianity")]

### Early Christian views and devotions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Michael_(archangel)&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Early Christian views and devotions")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/80/Stift_Rein_-_Bibliothek%2C_Antiphonale_Cisterciense%2C_Miniatur_Erzengel_Michael.jpg/250px-Stift_Rein_-_Bibliothek%2C_Antiphonale_Cisterciense%2C_Miniatur_Erzengel_Michael.jpg)](/wiki/File:Stift_Rein_-_Bibliothek,_Antiphonale_Cisterciense,_Miniatur_Erzengel_Michael.jpg)

*St. Michael weighing souls during the [Last Judgement](/wiki/Last_Judgement "Last Judgement")*, *Antiphonale Cisterciense* (15th century), Abbey Bibliotheca, [Rein Abbey, Austria](/wiki/Rein_Abbey,_Austria "Rein Abbey, Austria")

From the 2nd-century onwards there exists evidence attesting early Christian identifications of Michael as identical with the Christ particularly in works concerned with or by non-[proto-orthdox](/wiki/Proto-orthodox_Christianity "Proto-orthodox Christianity") groups such [Pseudo-Cyprian](/wiki/Pseudo-Cyprian "Pseudo-Cyprian")'s *De Centesima*, [Epiphanius](/wiki/Epiphanius_of_Salamis "Epiphanius of Salamis")' description of the [Ebionites](/wiki/Ebionites "Ebionites") and the sect of the [Elcasaites](/wiki/Elcesaites "Elcesaites"), [Tertullian](/wiki/Tertullian "Tertullian")'s attack on [Valentinian](/wiki/Valentinianism "Valentinianism") Christology, and certain magical texts, especially the [Testament of Solomon](/wiki/Testament_of_Solomon "Testament of Solomon").[[33]](#cite_note-34) The [Shepherd of Hermas](/wiki/The_Shepherd_of_Hermas "The Shepherd of Hermas"), possibly [Oxyrhynchus Papyrus](/wiki/Oxyrhynchus_Papyri "Oxyrhynchus Papyri") 1152, the [Gospel of the Hebrews](/wiki/Gospel_of_the_Hebrews "Gospel of the Hebrews"), the [pseudo-Clementine writings](/wiki/Clementine_literature "Clementine literature"), and Epiphanius' *[Panarion](/wiki/Panarion "Panarion")*[[b]](#cite_note-35) appear to share this view.[[34]](#cite_note-36) Other 2nd- and 3rd-century texts show that identifying Christ with an angel was not uncommon at the time—these include the [Gospel of Thomas](/wiki/Gospel_of_Thomas "Gospel of Thomas"), the [Gospel of Nicodemus](/wiki/Gospel_of_Nicodemus "Gospel of Nicodemus"), the [Testament of Dan](/wiki/Testaments_of_the_Twelve_Patriarchs "Testaments of the Twelve Patriarchs"), and the [Epistle to Diognetus](/wiki/Epistle_to_Diognetus "Epistle to Diognetus").[[35]](#cite_note-37) These beliefs likely derived from similar pre-Christian Jewish beliefs, such as those found in the [Prayer of Joseph](/wiki/Prayer_of_Joseph "Prayer of Joseph") and the beliefs of the [Magarites](/wiki/Magarites "Magarites").[[36]](#cite_note-38)

Epiphanius of Salamis (c. 310 – c. 320 – 403) in his Coptic-Arabic *Hexaemeron* additionally referred to Michael as a replacement of [Satan](/wiki/Satan "Satan"). Accordingly, after Satan fell, Michael was appointed to the function Satan served when he was still one of the noble angels.[[37]](#cite_note-39)

Byzantine tradition attributed two Bosporus-shore sanctuaries of the Archangel Michael to [Constantine](/wiki/Constantine_the_Great "Constantine the Great"). The first at Hestia/Anaplous—plausibly in the area of modern [Arnavutköy](/wiki/Arnavutk%C3%B6y_(district) "Arnavutköy (district)"), about 35 stadia by sea and over 70 by land from [Constantinople](/wiki/Constantinople "Constantinople"). The second at Sosthenion, commonly identified with modern [İstinye](/wiki/%C4%B0stinye "İstinye"). [Sozomen](/wiki/Sozomen "Sozomen") is the earliest witness for the Hestia site, while [John Malalas](/wiki/John_Malalas "John Malalas") places a Michaelion at Sosthenion and embeds it in a foundation legend that repurposes a pre-existing pagan shrine tied to the [Argonauts](/wiki/Argonauts "Argonauts"). Later sources often use "Anaplous" for the wider western Bosporus shore, which helps explain why Hestia/Anaplous and Sosthenion are sometimes conflated in the literature.[[38]](#cite_note-40)[[39]](#cite_note-41) He was [syncretically](/wiki/Syncretism "Syncretism") identified with the attributes of the god [Attis](/wiki/Attis "Attis") in Phrygia and thus associated with pre-Christian healing springs in [Phrygia](/wiki/Phrygia "Phrygia"), his main cult site was in [Chonae](/wiki/Colossae "Colossae").[[40]](#cite_note-42)[[41]](#cite_note-43)

A painting of the Archangel slaying a serpent became a major art piece at the *Michaelion* after Constantine defeated [Licinius](/wiki/Licinius "Licinius") near there in 324. This contributed to the standard [iconography](/wiki/Iconography "Iconography") that developed of the Archangel Michael as a [warrior saint](/wiki/Warrior_saint "Warrior saint") slaying a dragon.[[42]](#cite_note-Richard-44) The Michaelion was a magnificent church and in time became a model for hundreds of other churches in [Eastern Christianity](/wiki/Eastern_Christianity "Eastern Christianity"); these spread devotions to the Archangel.[[43]](#cite_note-45)

In the fourth century, [Saint Basil the Great](/wiki/Basil_the_Great "Basil the Great")'s homily (*De Angelis*) placed Saint Michael over all the angels. He was called "Archangel" because he heralds other angels, the title Ἀρχαγγέλος (*archangelos*) applied to him in [Jude](/wiki/Epistle_of_Jude "Epistle of Jude") 1:9.[[44]](#cite_note-Cathenc-46) Into the sixth century, the view of Michael as a healer continued in [Rome](/wiki/Rome "Rome"); after a plague, the sick slept at night in the church of *[Castel Sant'Angelo](/wiki/Castel_Sant%27Angelo "Castel Sant'Angelo")* (dedicated to him for saving Rome), waiting for his manifestation.[[45]](#cite_note-Butler320-47)

In the sixth century, the growth of devotions to Michael in the [Western Church](/wiki/Western_Church "Western Church") was expressed by the feasts dedicated to him, as recorded in the [Leonine Sacramentary](/wiki/Leonine_Sacramentary "Leonine Sacramentary"). The seventh-century [Gelasian Sacramentary](/wiki/Gelasian_Sacramentary "Gelasian Sacramentary") included the feast *"S. Michaelis Archangeli"*, as did the eighth-century [Gregorian Sacramentary](/wiki/Sacramentary#The_Gregorian_Sacramentary "Sacramentary"). Some of these documents refer to a *Basilica Archangeli* (no longer extant) on [via Salaria](/wiki/Via_Salaria "Via Salaria") in Rome.[[44]](#cite_note-Cathenc-46)

The [angelology](/wiki/Angelology "Angelology") of [Pseudo-Dionysius](/wiki/Pseudo-Dionysius_the_Areopagite "Pseudo-Dionysius the Areopagite"), which was widely read as of the sixth century, gave Michael a rank in the [hierarchy of angels](/wiki/Hierarchy_of_angels "Hierarchy of angels"). Later, in the thirteenth century, others such as [Bonaventure](/wiki/Bonaventure "Bonaventure") believed him to be Prince of the [Seraphim](/wiki/Seraphim "Seraphim"), the first of the nine angelic orders. According to [Thomas Aquinas](/wiki/Thomas_Aquinas "Thomas Aquinas") (*[Summa](/wiki/Summa_Theologica "Summa Theologica")* Ia. 113.3), he is Prince of the last and lowest choir, the Angels.[[44]](#cite_note-Cathenc-46)[[46]](#cite_note-48)

### Catholicism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Michael_(archangel)&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Catholicism")]

For Roman Catholic views and prayers, see [Saint Michael (Roman Catholic)](/wiki/Saint_Michael_(Roman_Catholic) "Saint Michael (Roman Catholic)").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2e/Second_class_Relic_stone_of_Saint_Michael_the_Archangel.jpg/250px-Second_class_Relic_stone_of_Saint_Michael_the_Archangel.jpg)](/wiki/File:Second_class_Relic_stone_of_Saint_Michael_the_Archangel.jpg)

Second-class relic stone of Saint Michael the Archangel from Monte Gargano, Italy

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e8/Jacopo_vignali%2C_san_michele_arcangelo_libera_le_anime_del_purgatorio.jpg/250px-Jacopo_vignali%2C_san_michele_arcangelo_libera_le_anime_del_purgatorio.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jacopo_vignali,_san_michele_arcangelo_libera_le_anime_del_purgatorio.jpg)

*[Archangel Michael frees souls from purgatory](/wiki/Saint_Michael_(Roman_Catholic)#At_the_hour_of_death "Saint Michael (Roman Catholic)")*, by [Jacopo Vignali](/wiki/Jacopo_Vignali "Jacopo Vignali"), 17th century

[Catholics](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") often refer to Michael as “Holy Michael, the Archangel”[[47]](#cite_note-49) or “Saint Michael”. He is generally referred to in Christian [litanies](/wiki/Litany "Litany") as “Saint Michael”, as in the [Litany of the Saints](/wiki/Litany_of_the_Saints "Litany of the Saints"). In the shortened version said at the [Easter Vigil](/wiki/Easter_Vigil "Easter Vigil"), he alone of the angels and archangels is mentioned by name, omitting Saints [Gabriel](/wiki/Gabriel "Gabriel") and [Raphael](/wiki/Raphael_(archangel) "Raphael (archangel)").[[48]](#cite_note-50)

In [Roman Catholic teachings](/wiki/Saint_Michael_(Roman_Catholic) "Saint Michael (Roman Catholic)"), Saint Michael has four main roles or offices.[[44]](#cite_note-Cathenc-46) His first role is the leader of the Army of God and the leader of celestial forces in triumphing over the powers of Hell.[[49]](#cite_note-Donna60-51) He is viewed as the angelic model for the virtues of the “spiritual warrior”, his conflict with evil taken as “the battle within”.[[50]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStarr20072-52)

The second and third roles of Michael in Catholic teachings deal with death. In his second role, he is the angel of death, [carrying the souls](/wiki/Psychopomp "Psychopomp") of Christians to Heaven. Catholic prayers often refer to this role of Michael. In his third role, he weighs souls on his perfectly balanced scales, a common object he holds in art.[[51]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStarr200739-53)

In his fourth role, Saint Michael, the special patron of the Chosen People in the Old Testament, is also Guardian of the Church. Saint Michael was revered by the [military orders](/wiki/Military_orders_of_the_Crusades "Military orders of the Crusades") of knights during the [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages_in_history "Middle Ages in history"). The names of villages around the [Bay of Biscay](/wiki/Bay_of_Biscay "Bay of Biscay") reflect that history. Moreover, doubtless for the same motive he was considered the patron saint of a number of cities and countries.[[52]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEButler1821117-54)[[53]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-55)

Catholic tradition includes also elements such as the *[Prayer to Saint Michael](/wiki/Prayer_to_Saint_Michael "Prayer to Saint Michael")*, which specifically asks the saint to “defend” the faithful from evil.[[54]](#cite_note-56)[[55]](#cite_note-57)[[56]](#cite_note-58) The *[Chaplet of Saint Michael](/wiki/Chaplet_of_Saint_Michael "Chaplet of Saint Michael")* consists of nine salutations, one for each choir of angels.[[57]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBall2003123-59)[[58]](#cite_note-60)

#### Saint Michael the Archangel prayer

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Michael_(archangel)&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Saint Michael the Archangel prayer")]

Main article: [Prayer to Saint Michael § In the Leonine Prayers](/wiki/Prayer_to_Saint_Michael#In_the_Leonine_Prayers "Prayer to Saint Michael")

> Sancte Míchael Archángele,   
> defénde nos in próelio;  
> contra nequítiam et insídias diáboli esto praesídium.  
> Imperet illi Deus, súpplices deprecámur,  
> tuque, Prínceps milítiae caeléstis,  
> Sátanam aliósque spíritus malígnos,  
> qui ad perditiónem animárum pervagántur in mundo,  
> divína virtúte, in inférnum detrúde.[[59]](#cite_note-bollettino-61)

Translation:
> Saint Michael the Archangel,   
> defend us in battle.  
> Be our protection against the wickedness and snares of the Devil.   
> May God rebuke him, we humbly pray,   
> and do thou, O Prince of the heavenly hosts,   
> by the power of God, thrust into hell Satan,   
> and all the evil spirits, who prowl about the world   
> seeking the ruin of souls.[[60]](#cite_note-62)

### Eastern and Oriental Orthodoxy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Michael_(archangel)&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Eastern and Oriental Orthodoxy")]

The [Eastern Orthodox](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox "Eastern Orthodox") accord Michael the title *Archistrategos*, or “Supreme Commander of the Heavenly Hosts”.[[61]](#cite_note-63) The Eastern Orthodox pray to their [guardian angels](/wiki/Guardian_angel "Guardian angel") and above all, to Michael and Gabriel.[[62]](#cite_note-64)

The Eastern Orthodox have always had strong devotions to angels. In contemporary times, they are referred to by the term of “Bodiless Powers”.[[63]](#cite_note-EOEnc-65) A number of feasts dedicated to Archangel Michael are celebrated by the Eastern Orthodox throughout the year.[[63]](#cite_note-EOEnc-65)

Archangel Michael is mentioned in a number of Eastern Orthodox hymns and prayer, and his icons are widely used within Eastern Orthodox churches.[[64]](#cite_note-B16-66) In many Eastern Orthodox [icons](/wiki/Icon "Icon"), Christ is accompanied by a number of angels, Michael being a predominant figure among them.[[64]](#cite_note-B16-66)

In [Russia](/wiki/Russia "Russia"), many monasteries, cathedrals, court and merchant churches are dedicated to the Chief Commander Michael; most Russian cities have a church or chapel dedicated to the Archangel Michael.[[65]](#cite_note-67)[[66]](#cite_note-68)

In Ukraine, the Archangel Michael is the patron saint of the capital city, Kyiv. He became popular since the time of [Prince Vsevolod](/wiki/Vsevolod_I_of_Kiev "Vsevolod I of Kiev") of Kievan Rus'.

While in the [Serbian Orthodox Church](/wiki/Serbian_Orthodox_Church "Serbian Orthodox Church") [Saint Sava](/wiki/Saint_Sava "Saint Sava") has a special role as the establisher of its autocephaly and the largest Belgrade church is devoted to him, the capital [Belgrade](/wiki/Belgrade "Belgrade")'s Orthodox [cathedral](/wiki/Cathedral "Cathedral"), the [see church](/wiki/St._Michael%27s_Cathedral,_Belgrade "St. Michael's Cathedral, Belgrade") of the patriarch, is devoted to Archangel Michael (in Serbian: *Арханђел Михаило* / *Arhanđel Mihailo*).

The place of Michael in the [Coptic Orthodox Church of Alexandria](/wiki/Coptic_Orthodox_Church_of_Alexandria "Coptic Orthodox Church of Alexandria") is as a [saintly intercessor](/wiki/Intercession_of_saints "Intercession of saints"). He is the one who presents to God the prayers of the just, who accompanies the souls of the dead to heaven, who defeats the devil. He is celebrated liturgically on the 12th of each [Coptic month](/wiki/Coptic_calendar "Coptic calendar").[[67]](#cite_note-69) In [Alexandria](/wiki/Alexandria "Alexandria"), a church was dedicated to him in the early fourth century on the 12th of the month of [Paoni](/wiki/Paoni "Paoni"). The 12th of the month of [Hathor](/wiki/Hathor_(month) "Hathor (month)") is the celebration of Michael's appointment in heaven, where Michael became the chief of the angels.[[68]](#cite_note-70)

### Protestantism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Michael_(archangel)&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Protestantism")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a1/West_Window%2C_Church_of_the_Good_Shepherd_%28Rosemont%2C_Pennsylvania%29.jpg/250px-West_Window%2C_Church_of_the_Good_Shepherd_%28Rosemont%2C_Pennsylvania%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:West_Window,_Church_of_the_Good_Shepherd_(Rosemont,_Pennsylvania).jpg)

West window showing Michael in armour, Episcopal [Church of the Good Shepherd (Rosemont, Pennsylvania)](/wiki/Church_of_the_Good_Shepherd_(Rosemont,_Pennsylvania) "Church of the Good Shepherd (Rosemont, Pennsylvania)") United States; memorial to the dead of the First World War

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c9/Erzengel_Michael-Statue_%C3%BCber_dem_Portal_der_St._Michaeliskirche_Hamburg.jpg/250px-Erzengel_Michael-Statue_%C3%BCber_dem_Portal_der_St._Michaeliskirche_Hamburg.jpg)](/wiki/File:Erzengel_Michael-Statue_%C3%BCber_dem_Portal_der_St._Michaeliskirche_Hamburg.jpg)

Statue at [St. Michael's Church, Hamburg](/wiki/St._Michael%27s_Church,_Hamburg "St. Michael's Church, Hamburg")

Protestants recognize Michael as an archangel. The [Anglican](/wiki/Anglican "Anglican") and [Methodist](/wiki/Methodist "Methodist") traditions recognize four archangels: Michael, [Raphael](/wiki/Raphael_(archangel) "Raphael (archangel)"), [Gabriel](/wiki/Gabriel "Gabriel"), and [Uriel](/wiki/Uriel "Uriel").[[69]](#cite_note-Armentrout2000-71)[[70]](#cite_note-72) The controversial Anglican bishop [Robert Clayton](/wiki/Robert_Clayton_(bishop) "Robert Clayton (bishop)") (d. 1758) proposed that Michael was the [Logos](/wiki/Logos_(Christianity) "Logos (Christianity)") and Gabriel the [Holy Spirit](/wiki/Holy_Spirit_(Christianity) "Holy Spirit (Christianity)").[[71]](#cite_note-73) Controversy over Clayton's views led the government to order his prosecution, but he died before his scheduled examination.[[72]](#cite_note-74)[[73]](#cite_note-75)

The [Lutheran Churches](/wiki/Lutheran_Church "Lutheran Church") of [St. Michael's Church, Hamburg](/wiki/St._Michael%27s_Church,_Hamburg "St. Michael's Church, Hamburg") and [St. Michael's Church, Hildesheim](/wiki/St._Michael%27s_Church,_Hildesheim "St. Michael's Church, Hildesheim") are named for Michael. In [Bach](/wiki/Johann_Sebastian_Bach "Johann Sebastian Bach")'s time, the annual feast of Michael and All the Angels on 29 September was regularly celebrated with a festive service in Lutheran churches, for which Bach composed several cantatas, for example the [chorale cantata](/wiki/Chorale_cantata_(Bach) "Chorale cantata (Bach)") *[Herr Gott, dich loben alle wir, BWV 130](/wiki/Herr_Gott,_dich_loben_alle_wir,_BWV_130 "Herr Gott, dich loben alle wir, BWV 130")* in 1724, [*Es erhub sich ein Streit*, BWV 19](/wiki/Es_erhub_sich_ein_Streit,_BWV_19 "Es erhub sich ein Streit, BWV 19"), in 1726 and [*Man singet mit Freuden vom Sieg*, BWV 149](/wiki/Man_singet_mit_Freuden_vom_Sieg,_BWV_149 "Man singet mit Freuden vom Sieg, BWV 149"), in 1728 or 1729.

Like the aforementioned early Christian groups, many Protestant theologians identify a relationship, (e.g. [typological](/wiki/Typology_(theology) "Typology (theology)") or identical), between Michael and Christ in some[[c]](#cite_note-76)[[74]](#cite_note-77) or all Biblical mentions of the name[[75]](#cite_note-78)[[76]](#cite_note-79) including:

[Martin Luther](/wiki/Martin_Luther "Martin Luther")[[77]](#cite_note-80)[[78]](#cite_note-81) [Ernst Wilhelm Hengstenberg](/wiki/Ernst_Wilhelm_Hengstenberg "Ernst Wilhelm Hengstenberg"),[[79]](#cite_note-82) [Andrew Willet](/wiki/Andrew_Willet "Andrew Willet")[[80]](#cite_note-83) [Herman Witsius](/wiki/Hermann_Witsius "Hermann Witsius")[[81]](#cite_note-84) [W. L. Alexander](/wiki/William_Lindsay_Alexander "William Lindsay Alexander"), Jacobus Ode,[[82]](#cite_note-85) [Campegius Vitringa](/wiki/Campegius_Vitringa "Campegius Vitringa"),[[83]](#cite_note-86) [Philip Melanchthon](/wiki/Philip_Melanchthon "Philip Melanchthon"),[[84]](#cite_note-87) [Hugh Broughton](/wiki/Hugh_Broughton "Hugh Broughton"),[[85]](#cite_note-88) [Franciscus Junius](/wiki/Franciscus_Junius_(the_elder) "Franciscus Junius (the elder)"),[[86]](#cite_note-89) [Hävernick](/wiki/Heinrich_Andreas_Christoph_Havernick "Heinrich Andreas Christoph Havernick")[[87]](#cite_note-90) [Amandus Polanus](/wiki/Amandus_Polanus "Amandus Polanus"),[[88]](#cite_note-91) [Johannes Oecolampadius](/wiki/Johannes_Oecolampadius "Johannes Oecolampadius"),[[89]](#cite_note-92) [Samuel Horsely](/wiki/Samuel_Horsley "Samuel Horsley"),[[90]](#cite_note-93) William Kincaid[[91]](#cite_note-94) [John Calvin](/wiki/John_Calvin "John Calvin")[[92]](#cite_note-95) [Isaac Watts](/wiki/Isaac_Watts "Isaac Watts"),[[93]](#cite_note-96) [John Brown](/wiki/John_Brown_of_Haddington "John Brown of Haddington"),[[94]](#cite_note-97) and James Wood.[[95]](#cite_note-98)

[Charles Spurgeon](/wiki/Charles_Haddon_Spurgeon "Charles Haddon Spurgeon") once stated that Jesus is "the true Michael"[[96]](#cite_note-99) and "the only Archangel".[[97]](#cite_note-100)

[John Gill](/wiki/John_Gill_(theologian) "John Gill (theologian)") comments on Jude 9, "'Yet Michael the archangel ...' By whom is meant, not a created angel, but an eternal one, the Lord Jesus Christ ..."[[98]](#cite_note-101)

#### Seventh-day Adventists

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Michael_(archangel)&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Seventh-day Adventists")]

See also: [Seventh-day Adventist, beliefs about Michael](/wiki/Seventh-day_Adventist_theology#Christ_and_the_Archangel_Michael "Seventh-day Adventist theology") and [Pre-existence of Christ](/wiki/Pre-existence_of_Christ "Pre-existence of Christ")

[Seventh-day Adventists](/wiki/Seventh-day_Adventist_Church "Seventh-day Adventist Church") believe that "Michael" is but one of the many titles applied to the pre-existent Christ, or Son of God. According to Adventists, such a view does not in any way conflict with the belief in the full deity and eternal preexistence of Jesus Christ, nor does it in the least disparage his person and work.[[99]](#cite_note-102) According to Adventist theology, Michael was considered the "eternal Word", and the one by whom all things were created. The Word was then born incarnate as Jesus.[[100]](#cite_note-103)

They believe that name "Michael" signifies "One Who Is Like God" and that as the "Archangel" or "chief or head of the angels" he led the angels and thus the statement in [Revelation 12:7–9](https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Bible_(American_Standard)/Revelation#12:7 "s:Bible (American Standard)/Revelation") identifies/refers to Jesus as Michael.[[101]](#cite_note-104)

### Restorationism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Michael_(archangel)&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Restorationism")]

#### Jehovah's Witnesses

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Michael_(archangel)&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Jehovah's Witnesses")]

See also: [Jehovah's Witnesses beliefs about Jesus](/wiki/Jehovah%27s_witnesses#Jesus "Jehovah's witnesses")

[Jehovah's Witnesses](/wiki/Jehovah%27s_Witnesses "Jehovah's Witnesses") believe Michael to be another name for [Jesus in heaven](/wiki/Pre-existence_of_Christ "Pre-existence of Christ"), in his pre-human and post-resurrection existence.[[102]](#cite_note-autogenerated218-105) They say the definite article at Jude 9[[103]](#cite_note-106)—referring to "Michael the archangel"—identifies Michael as the only archangel. They consider Michael to be synonymous with Christ, described at 1 Thessalonians 4:16[[104]](#cite_note-107) as descending "with a cry of command, with the voice of an archangel, and with the sound of the trumpet".[[105]](#cite_note-108)[[106]](#cite_note-109)[[107]](#cite_note-110)

They believe the prominent roles assigned to Michael at Daniel 12:1,[[108]](#cite_note-111) Revelation 12:7,[[109]](#cite_note-112) Revelation 19:14,[[110]](#cite_note-113) and Revelation 16[[111]](#cite_note-114) are identical to Jesus' roles, being the one chosen to lead God's people and as the only one who "stands up", identifying the two as the same spirit being. Because they identify Michael with Jesus, he is therefore considered the first and greatest of all God's heavenly sons, God's chief messenger, who takes the lead in vindicating God's [sovereignty](/wiki/Sovereignty "Sovereignty"), sanctifying his name, fighting the wicked forces of Satan and protecting God's covenant people on earth.[[112]](#cite_note-115) Jehovah's Witnesses also identify Michael with the "[Angel of the Lord](/wiki/Angel_of_the_Lord "Angel of the Lord")" who led and protected the Israelites in the wilderness.[[113]](#cite_note-116) Their earliest teachings stated that Archangel Michael was not to be worshipped.[[114]](#cite_note-117)

#### The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Michael_(archangel)&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints")]

See also: [Beliefs and practices of The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints](/wiki/Beliefs_and_practices_of_The_Church_of_Jesus_Christ_of_Latter-day_Saints "Beliefs and practices of The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints")

Members of [the Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints](/wiki/The_Church_of_Jesus_Christ_of_Latter-day_Saints "The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints") believe that Michael is [Adam](/wiki/Adam_and_Eve_(LDS_Church) "Adam and Eve (LDS Church)"), the [Ancient of Days](/wiki/Ancient_of_Days "Ancient of Days") (Dan. 7), a prince, and the patriarch of the human family. They also hold that Michael assisted [Jehovah](/wiki/Jehovah "Jehovah") (the [pre-mortal](/wiki/Pre-existence#Latter_Day_Saints "Pre-existence") form of [Jesus](/wiki/Jesus "Jesus")) in the creation of the world under the direction of [God the Father](/wiki/God_the_Father "God the Father") ([Elohim](/wiki/Elohim#Latter_Day_Saint_movement "Elohim")); under the direction of the Father, Michael also cast Satan out of heaven.[[115]](#cite_note-118)[[116]](#cite_note-119)[[117]](#cite_note-120)[[118]](#cite_note-121)

## Islam

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Michael_(archangel)&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Islam")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/af/Muhammad_ibn_Muhammad_Shakir_Ruzmah-%27i_Nathani_-_The_Archangel_Michael_-_Walters_W65946B_-_Full_Page.jpg/250px-Muhammad_ibn_Muhammad_Shakir_Ruzmah-%27i_Nathani_-_The_Archangel_Michael_-_Walters_W65946B_-_Full_Page.jpg)](/wiki/File:Muhammad_ibn_Muhammad_Shakir_Ruzmah-%27i_Nathani_-_The_Archangel_Michael_-_Walters_W65946B_-_Full_Page.jpg)

Muhammad ibn Muhammad Shakir Ruzmah-'i Nathani - The Archangel Michael - Walters W65946B - Full Page

In Islam, Michael, or Mīkāʾīl,[[119]](#cite_note-King-122) is one of the four archangels along with [Jibril](/wiki/Gabriel#Islam "Gabriel") (Gabriel, whom he is often paired with), [ʾIsrāfīl](/wiki/Israfil "Israfil") (trumpeter angel) and [ʿAzrāʾīl](/wiki/Azrael "Azrael") (angel of death).[[120]](#cite_note-123) He is mentioned only once in the Quran, along with Gabriel in [Quran](/wiki/Quran "Quran") [2:98](https://www.perseus.tufts.edu/hopper/text?doc=Perseus%3Atext%3A2002.02.0006%3Asura%3D2%3Averse%3D98).[[121]](#cite_note-124) The verse is understood as a rejection of the claim of the Jews of Medina stating that Gabriel is the enemy of Michael.[[122]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENoegelWheeler2002218-125) In [hadith](/wiki/Hadith "Hadith") and [tafsir](/wiki/Tafsir "Tafsir"), the meaning of the term is occasionally interpreted as "ʿabd Allāh" (Servant of God).[[123]](#cite_note-126)

In further Islamic literature, Michael is associated with mercy. He asks [God](/wiki/God_in_Islam "God in Islam") for forgiveness for humans and is one of the first angels who obeyed God's orders to bow before [Adam](/wiki/Adam_in_Islam "Adam in Islam").[[124]](#cite_note-127)[[125]](#cite_note-britannica-128) From the tears of Michael, angels of mercy are created as his helpers.[[126]](#cite_note-129) Like Gabriel, with whom he is often mentioned together, Michael is also a messenger. While Gabriel delivers messages from heaven to humans, Michael delivers messages to the angelic world.[[127]](#cite_note-130) As the angel to effectuate God's providence he is also associated with natural phenomena and causes rain upon the lands.[[125]](#cite_note-britannica-128) The latter function is also attested among modern writers, such as [Sayyid Qutb](/wiki/Sayyid_Qutb "Sayyid Qutb").[[128]](#cite_note-131) Unlike Christian tradition, Michael is rarely portrayed as a warrior-angel, with a few references to the [Battle of Badr](/wiki/Battle_of_Badr "Battle of Badr") by [Suyuti](/wiki/Suyuti "Suyuti") as an exception.[[129]](#cite_note-132)

The [Miraj literature](/wiki/Isra_and_Miraj "Isra and Miraj") occasionally mentions both Gabriel and Michael as two angels who showed [Muhammad](/wiki/Muhammad_in_Islam "Muhammad in Islam") Paradise and hell.[[130]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECapmak20171042-133)[[131]](#cite_note-134) However, he does not feature prominently and some accounts do not mention him at all.[[132]](#cite_note-135) Prayers concerning Michael appear in some devotional literature, but usually in conjunction with the other three archangels.[[133]](#cite_note-136)[[130]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECapmak20171042-133) He is mentioned in a [Shia](/wiki/Shia_Islam "Shia Islam") [supplication](/wiki/Dua "Dua") (*dua*), reportedly handed down by the 6th [Imam Ja'far al-Sadiq](/wiki/Imam_Ja%27far_al-Sadiq "Imam Ja'far al-Sadiq"), in the prayers for blessings for the [Bearers of the Throne](/wiki/Bearers_of_the_Throne "Bearers of the Throne").[[134]](#cite_note-137)[[135]](#cite_note-138)[[136]](#cite_note-139)

## Bahá'í Faith

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Michael_(archangel)&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Bahá'í Faith")]

The archangel Michael seems to have never been mentioned publicly by [Baha'u'llah](/wiki/Baha%27u%27llah "Baha'u'llah"), ['Abdu'l-Baha](/wiki/%27Abdu%27l-Baha "'Abdu'l-Baha"), [Shoghi Effendi](/wiki/Shoghi_Effendi "Shoghi Effendi"), or even the [Universal House of Justice](/wiki/Universal_House_of_Justice "Universal House of Justice"). Bahá'í publications interpreting the [Book of Revelation](/wiki/Book_of_Revelation "Book of Revelation") from the [New Testament](/wiki/New_Testament "New Testament") say Baha'u'llah was a chief prince of Persia[[137]](#cite_note-140)[[138]](#cite_note-141) foretold as Michael who would win "final victory over the dragon". Or, Michael, "One like God", is thought to be Baha'u'llah, as archangel Michael is thought to be an emanation of [Hod](/wiki/Hod_(Kabbalah) "Hod (Kabbalah)") or "glory" in [Jewish Mysticism](/wiki/Jewish_Mysticism "Jewish Mysticism")[[139]](#cite_note-142) – because "Baha'u'llah" means splendor or glory of God.

## Feasts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Michael_(archangel)&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Feasts")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/47/S._Miguel_Arcanjo.jpg/250px-S._Miguel_Arcanjo.jpg)](/wiki/File:S._Miguel_Arcanjo.jpg)

Archangel Michael at a Portuguese feast in [Cabeceiras de Basto](/wiki/Cabeceiras_de_Basto "Cabeceiras de Basto")

In the [General Roman Calendar](/wiki/General_Roman_Calendar "General Roman Calendar"), the [Anglican Calendar of Saints](/wiki/Calendar_of_saints_(Church_of_England) "Calendar of saints (Church of England)"), and the [Lutheran Calendar of Saints](/wiki/Calendar_of_Saints_(Lutheran) "Calendar of Saints (Lutheran)"), the archangel's feast is celebrated on [Michaelmas](/wiki/Michaelmas "Michaelmas") Day, 29 September. The day is also considered the feast of Saints Michael, [Gabriel](/wiki/Gabriel "Gabriel"), and [Raphael](/wiki/Raphael_(archangel) "Raphael (archangel)"), in the General Roman Calendar and the Feast of Saint Michael and All Angels according to the Church of England.[[140]](#cite_note-143)

In the [Eastern Orthodox Church](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Church "Eastern Orthodox Church"), Saint Michael's principal feast day is 8 November (those that use the [Julian calendar](/wiki/Julian_calendar "Julian calendar") celebrate it on what in the [Gregorian calendar](/wiki/Gregorian_calendar "Gregorian calendar") is now 21 November), honouring him along with the rest of the *"Bodiless Powers of Heaven"* (i.e. [angels](/wiki/Angel "Angel")) as their Supreme Commander (Synaxis of the Archangel Michael and the Other Bodiless Powers), and the *[Miracle at Chonae](/wiki/Miracle_of_the_Archangel_Michael_at_Chonae#Miracle_of_the_Archangel_Michael "Miracle of the Archangel Michael at Chonae")* is commemorated on 6 September.[[141]](#cite_note-144)[[142]](#cite_note-145)

In the calendar of the [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England") [diocese of Truro](/wiki/Diocese_of_Truro "Diocese of Truro"), 8 May is the feast of *St. Michael, Protector of Cornwall*. The archangel Michael is one of the three patron saints of [Cornwall](/wiki/Cornwall "Cornwall").[[143]](#cite_note-146) The feast of the Appearing of S. Michael the Archangel is observed by Anglo-Catholics on 8 May.[[144]](#cite_note-147) From medieval times until 1960 it was also observed on that day in the Roman Catholic Church; the feast commemorates the archangel's apparition on [Mount Gargano in Italy](/wiki/Sanctuary_of_Monte_Sant%27Angelo "Sanctuary of Monte Sant'Angelo").[[145]](#cite_note-148)

In the [Coptic Orthodox Church](/wiki/Coptic_Orthodox_Church "Coptic Orthodox Church"), the main feast day in 12 [Hathor](/wiki/Hathor_(month) "Hathor (month)") and 12 [Paoni](/wiki/Paoni "Paoni"), and he is celebrated liturgically on the 12th of each Coptic month.

[Feast of Saint Michael and All Angels](/wiki/Feast_of_Saint_Michael_and_All_Angels "Feast of Saint Michael and All Angels") is [commemorated](/wiki/Calendar_of_saints "Calendar of saints") on 29 September in [ROCOR](/wiki/ROCOR "ROCOR") [Western Rite](/wiki/Western_Rite_Orthodoxy "Western Rite Orthodoxy").[[146]](#cite_note-149)

[Apparition](/wiki/Vision_(spirituality) "Vision (spirituality)") of Saint Michael in 492 on [Mount Gargano](/wiki/Sanctuary_of_Monte_Sant%27Angelo "Sanctuary of Monte Sant'Angelo") is [commemorated](/wiki/Calendar_of_saints "Calendar of saints") on 8 May[[147]](#cite_note-150) and [Dedication of Saint Michael the Archangel](/wiki/Michaelmas "Michaelmas") is [commemorated](/wiki/Calendar_of_saints "Calendar of saints") on 29 September ([Antiochian Western Rite Vicariate](/wiki/Antiochian_Western_Rite_Vicariate "Antiochian Western Rite Vicariate")).[[148]](#cite_note-151)

[Dedication](/wiki/Dedication_of_churches "Dedication of churches") of Saint Michael [sanctuary](/wiki/Sanctuary "Sanctuary") [Mont Saint-Michel](/wiki/Mont_Saint-Michel_Abbey "Mont Saint-Michel Abbey") by [Saint Aubert of Avranches](/wiki/Aubert_of_Avranches "Aubert of Avranches") is commemorated on 16 October.[[149]](#cite_note-152)

On 7 April, the Oriental Orthodox Church commemorates the deliverance of [prophet Jeremiah](/wiki/Jeremiah "Jeremiah") from prison by Michael.[[150]](#cite_note-:0-153)

## Patronages and orders

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Michael_(archangel)&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Patronages and orders")]

In late [medieval Christianity](/wiki/Medieval_Christianity "Medieval Christianity"), Michael, together with [Saint George](/wiki/Saint_George "Saint George"), became the [patron saint](/wiki/Patron_saint "Patron saint") of [chivalry](/wiki/Chivalry "Chivalry") and is now also considered the patron saint of police officers, paramedics and the military.[[53]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-55)[[151]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBall2003586-154)

Since the victorious [Battle of Lechfeld](/wiki/Battle_of_Lechfeld "Battle of Lechfeld") against the Hungarians in 955, Michael was the patron saint of the [Holy Roman Empire](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Empire "Holy Roman Empire") and the Patron of the [Germans](/wiki/Germans "Germans").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/eb/Michael4.jpg/250px-Michael4.jpg)](/wiki/File:Michael4.jpg)

Statue of St Michael at the former seat of the [Bavarian](/wiki/House_of_Wittelsbach "House of Wittelsbach") [Military Order of Saint Michael](/wiki/Order_of_Saint_Michael_(Bavaria) "Order of Saint Michael (Bavaria)") in the [Electoral Palace, Bonn](/wiki/Electoral_Palace,_Bonn "Electoral Palace, Bonn"), Germany

In mid- to late fifteenth century, France was one of only four courts in [Western Christendom](/wiki/Western_Christianity "Western Christianity") without an order of knighthood.[[152]](#cite_note-Dacre-155) Later in the fifteenth century, [Jean Molinet](/wiki/Jean_Molinet "Jean Molinet") glorified the primordial feat of arms of the archangel as "the first deed of knighthood and chivalrous prowess that was ever achieved."[[153]](#cite_note-156) Thus Michael was the natural patron of the first [chivalric order](/wiki/Chivalric_order "Chivalric order") of France, the [Order of Saint Michael](/wiki/Order_of_Saint_Michael "Order of Saint Michael") of 1469.[[152]](#cite_note-Dacre-155) In the [British honours system](/wiki/British_honours_system "British honours system"), a chivalric order founded in 1818 is also named for these two saints, the [Order of St Michael and St George](/wiki/Order_of_St_Michael_and_St_George "Order of St Michael and St George") (*see also*: [Order of Saint Michael](/wiki/Order_of_Saint_Michael_(disambiguation) "Order of Saint Michael (disambiguation)")).[[154]](#cite_note-157)

Prior to 1878, the [Scapular of St. Michael the Archangel](/wiki/Scapular_of_Saint_Michael_the_Archangel "Scapular of Saint Michael the Archangel") could be worn as part of a Roman Catholic [Archconfraternity](/wiki/Archconfraternity "Archconfraternity"). Presently, enrollment is authorized as this [holy scapular](/wiki/Scapular_of_Our_Lady_of_Mount_Carmel "Scapular of Our Lady of Mount Carmel") remains as one of the 18 approved by the Church.

Apart from his being a patron of warriors, the sick and the suffering also consider Archangel Michael their patron saint.[[155]](#cite_note-158) Based on the legend of his eighth-century [apparition](/wiki/Vision_(spirituality) "Vision (spirituality)") at [Mont Saint-Michel](/wiki/Mont_Saint-Michel "Mont Saint-Michel"), France, the Archangel is the patron of [mariners](/wiki/Sailors "Sailors") in this famous sanctuary.[[44]](#cite_note-Cathenc-46) After the evangelisation of Germany, where mountains were often dedicated to pagan gods, Christians placed many mountains under the patronage of the Archangel, and numerous mountain chapels of St. Michael appeared all over Germany.[[44]](#cite_note-Cathenc-46)

Similarly, the Sanctuary of St. Michel (San Migel Aralarkoa), the oldest Christian building in Navarre (Spain), lies at the top of a hill on the [Aralar Range](/wiki/Aralar_Range "Aralar Range"), and harbours Carolingian remains. St. Michel is an ancient devotion of [Navarre](/wiki/Navarre "Navarre") and eastern [Gipuzkoa](/wiki/Gipuzkoa "Gipuzkoa"), revered by the [Basques](/wiki/History_of_the_Basques#Christianization "History of the Basques"), shrouded in legend, and held as a champion against paganism and heresy. It came to symbolize the defense of Catholicism, as well as Basque tradition and values during the early twentieth century.[[156]](#cite_note-159)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/69/Greater_coat_of_arms_of_the_City_of_Brussels.svg/250px-Greater_coat_of_arms_of_the_City_of_Brussels.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Greater_coat_of_arms_of_the_City_of_Brussels.svg)

[Coat of arms of Brussels](/wiki/Coat_of_arms_of_Brussels "Coat of arms of Brussels")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1a/COA_of_Kyiv_Kurovskyi.svg/250px-COA_of_Kyiv_Kurovskyi.svg.png)](/wiki/File:COA_of_Kyiv_Kurovskyi.svg)

[Coat of arms of Kyiv](/wiki/Coat_of_arms_of_Kyiv "Coat of arms of Kyiv")

He has been the patron saint of [Brussels](/wiki/Brussels "Brussels") since the Middle Ages.[[157]](#cite_note-160) The city of [Arkhangelsk](/wiki/Arkhangelsk "Arkhangelsk") in Russia is named for the Archangel. Ukraine and its capital [Kyiv](/wiki/Kyiv "Kyiv") also consider Michael their patron saint and protector.[[158]](#cite_note-Fairbairn-161)

In [Linlithgow](/wiki/Linlithgow "Linlithgow"), Scotland, St. Michael has been the patron saint of the town since the thirteenth century, with [St. Michael's Parish Church](/w/index.php?title=St._Michael%27s_Parish_Church&action=edit&redlink=1 "St. Michael's Parish Church (page does not exist)") being originally constructed in 1134.
Since the fourteenth century, Saint Michael has been the patron saint of [Dumfries](/wiki/Dumfries "Dumfries") in Scotland, where a church dedicated to him was built at the southern end of the town, on a mound overlooking the [River Nith](/wiki/River_Nith "River Nith").[[159]](#cite_note-162)

An [Anglican](/wiki/Anglican "Anglican") sisterhood dedicated to Saint Michael under the title of the [Community of St Michael and All Angels](/wiki/Community_of_St_Michael_and_All_Angels "Community of St Michael and All Angels") was founded in 1851.[[160]](#cite_note-163) The [Congregation of Saint Michael the Archangel](/wiki/Congregation_of_Saint_Michael_the_Archangel "Congregation of Saint Michael the Archangel") (CSMA), also known as the *Michaelite Fathers*, is a religious order of the Roman Catholic Church founded in 1897. The Canons Regular of the Order of St Michael the Archangel (OSM) are an Order of [professed](/wiki/Profession_(religious) "Profession (religious)") religious within the [Anglican Church in North America](/wiki/Anglican_Church_in_North_America "Anglican Church in North America"), the North American component of the [Anglican realignment](/wiki/Anglican_realignment "Anglican realignment") movement.[[161]](#cite_note-164)

The city of [Arkhangelsk](/wiki/Arkhangelsk "Arkhangelsk"), Russia, and the federal subject [Arkhangelsk Oblast](/wiki/Arkhangelsk_Oblast "Arkhangelsk Oblast") are named after Michael the Archangel.

In the United States military, Saint Michael is considered to be a patron of paratroopers and, in particular, the [82nd Airborne Division](/wiki/82nd_Airborne_Division "82nd Airborne Division").[[162]](#cite_note-165) One of the first battles where the unit first was combat christened is the [Battle of Saint-Mihiel](/wiki/Battle_of_Saint-Mihiel "Battle of Saint-Mihiel") during World War I.

The beret insignia of the French paratroopers is a winged arm grasping a dagger, representing Saint Michael.[[163]](#cite_note-166)
Saint Michael is the patronus of [9th Paratroopers Assault Regiment "Col Moschin"](/wiki/9th_Paratroopers_Assault_Regiment_%22Col_Moschin%22 "9th Paratroopers Assault Regiment \"Col Moschin\"") and the [Polizia di Stato](/wiki/Polizia_di_Stato "Polizia di Stato"). Saint Michael ([Croatian](/wiki/Croatian_language "Croatian language"): *Sveti Mihovil*) is patron of [Croatian Police](/wiki/Croatian_Police "Croatian Police") and [Croatian Army](/wiki/Croatian_Army "Croatian Army"), his feast day being also celebrated as the Police day in Croatia.[[164]](#cite_note-167)

## Legends

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Michael_(archangel)&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Legends")]

### Judaism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Michael_(archangel)&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Judaism")]

There is a legend which seems to be of Jewish origin, and which was adopted by the [Copts](/wiki/Copt "Copt"), to the effect that Michael was first sent by God to bring [Nebuchadnezzar](/wiki/Nebuchadnezzar "Nebuchadnezzar") (c. 600 BC) against Jerusalem, and that Michael was afterward very active in freeing his nation from Babylonian captivity.[[165]](#cite_note-168)
According to midrash [Genesis Rabbah](/wiki/Genesis_Rabbah "Genesis Rabbah"), Michael saved [Hananiah](/wiki/Shadrach,_Meshach,_and_Abednego "Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego") and his companions from the [fiery furnace](/wiki/Shadrach,_Meshach,_and_Abednego "Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego").[[166]](#cite_note-169) Michael was active in the time of [Esther](/wiki/Esther "Esther"): "The more [Haman](/wiki/Haman_(Bible) "Haman (Bible)") accused Israel on earth, the more Michael defended Israel in heaven".[[167]](#cite_note-170) It was Michael who reminded [Ahasuerus](/wiki/Ahasuerus "Ahasuerus") that he was [Mordecai](/wiki/Mordecai "Mordecai")'s debtor;[[168]](#cite_note-171) and there is a legend that Michael appeared to the high priest [Hyrcanus](/wiki/John_Hyrcanus "John Hyrcanus"), promising him assistance.[[169]](#cite_note-172)

According to *[Legends of the Jews](/wiki/Legends_of_the_Jews "Legends of the Jews")*, archangel Michael was the chief of a band of angels who questioned God's decision to create man on Earth. The entire band of angels, except for Michael, was then consumed by fire.[[170]](#cite_note-ginzberg_michael-173)

### Christianity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Michael_(archangel)&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Christianity")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c9/Michael_Miracle_Icon_Sinai_12th_century.jpg/250px-Michael_Miracle_Icon_Sinai_12th_century.jpg)](/wiki/File:Michael_Miracle_Icon_Sinai_12th_century.jpg)

A 12th-century [icon](/wiki/Icon "Icon") of the *[Miracle at Chonae](/wiki/Miracle_of_the_Archangel_Michael_at_Chonae "Miracle of the Archangel Michael at Chonae")*, from [Saint Catherine's Monastery](/wiki/Saint_Catherine%27s_Monastery "Saint Catherine's Monastery"), [Mount Sinai](/wiki/Mount_Sinai "Mount Sinai")

The [Eastern Orthodox Church](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Church "Eastern Orthodox Church") celebrates the [Miracle at Chonae](/wiki/Miracle_of_the_Archangel_Michael_at_Chonae "Miracle of the Archangel Michael at Chonae") on September 6.[[171]](#cite_note-174) The pious legend surrounding the event states that [John the Apostle](/wiki/John_the_Apostle "John the Apostle"), when preaching nearby, foretold the appearance of Michael at Cheretopa near [Lake Salda](/wiki/Lake_Salda "Lake Salda"), where a healing spring appeared soon after the Apostle left; in gratitude for the healing of his daughter, one pilgrim built a church on the site.[[172]](#cite_note-175) Local pagans, who are described as jealous of the healing power of the spring and the church, attempt to drown the church by redirecting the river, but the Archangel, "in the likeness of a column of fire", split the bedrock to open up a new bed for the stream, directing the flow away from the church.[[173]](#cite_note-176) The legend is supposed to have predated the actual events, but the fifth- to seventh-century texts that refer to the miracle at Chonae formed the basis of specific paradigms for "properly approaching" angelic intermediaries for more effective prayers within the Christian culture.[[174]](#cite_note-Peers-177)

There is a late-fifth-century legend in [Cornwall](/wiki/Cornwall "Cornwall"), England, that the Archangel appeared to fishermen on [St Michael's Mount](/wiki/St_Michael%27s_Mount "St Michael's Mount").[[175]](#cite_note-Cornwall-178) According to author Richard Freeman Johnson, this legend is likely a nationalistic twist to a myth.[[175]](#cite_note-Cornwall-178) Cornish legends also hold that the mount itself was constructed by giants[[176]](#cite_note-179) and that [King Arthur](/wiki/King_Arthur "King Arthur") battled a giant there.[[177]](#cite_note-180)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5b/St._Michael_the_Archangel_of_Bacoor.jpg/250px-St._Michael_the_Archangel_of_Bacoor.jpg)](/wiki/File:St._Michael_the_Archangel_of_Bacoor.jpg)

An 18th-century statue of a triumphant Saint Michael, enshrined as the patron of [Bacoor, Cavite](/wiki/Bacoor,_Cavite "Bacoor, Cavite"), [Philippines](/wiki/Philippines "Philippines"). The town *fiesta* was originally on May 8, the Feast of the Apparition at Mount Gargano.

The legend of the apparition of the Archangel at around AD 490 at a secluded hilltop cave on [Monte Gargano](/wiki/Monte_Gargano "Monte Gargano") in Italy gained a following among the [Lombards](/wiki/Lombards "Lombards") in the immediate period thereafter, and by the eighth century, pilgrims arrived from as far away as England.[[178]](#cite_note-181) The [Tridentine calendar](/wiki/Tridentine_calendar "Tridentine calendar") included a feast of the apparition on 8 May, the date of the 663 victory over the [Greek](/wiki/Greece "Greece") [Neapolitans](/wiki/Naples "Naples") that the Lombards of [Manfredonia](/wiki/Manfredonia "Manfredonia") attributed to Saint Michael.[[44]](#cite_note-Cathenc-46) The feast [remained](/wiki/General_Roman_Calendar_of_1954 "General Roman Calendar of 1954") in the Roman liturgical calendar until removed in the [revision](/wiki/General_Roman_Calendar_of_1960 "General Roman Calendar of 1960") of [Pope John XXIII](/wiki/Pope_John_XXIII "Pope John XXIII"). The [Sanctuary of Monte Sant'Angelo](/wiki/Sanctuary_of_Monte_Sant%27Angelo "Sanctuary of Monte Sant'Angelo") at Gargano is a major Catholic pilgrimage site.

According to Roman legends, Archangel Michael appeared with a sword over the [mausoleum](/wiki/Mausoleum "Mausoleum") of [Hadrian](/wiki/Hadrian "Hadrian") while a devastating plague persisted in Rome, in apparent answer to the prayers of Pope [Gregory I the Great](/wiki/Gregory_I_the_Great "Gregory I the Great") (c. 590–604) that the plague should cease. After the plague ended, in honor of the occasion, the pope called the mausoleum *"[Castel Sant'Angelo](/wiki/Castel_Sant%27Angelo "Castel Sant'Angelo")"* (Castle of the Holy Angel), the name by which it is still known.[[45]](#cite_note-Butler320-47)

According to [Norman](/wiki/Normandy "Normandy") legend, Michael is said to have appeared to [St Aubert](/wiki/St_Aubert "St Aubert"), [Bishop of Avranches](/wiki/Bishop_of_Avranches "Bishop of Avranches"), in 708, giving instruction to build a church on the rocky islet now known as [Mont Saint-Michel](/wiki/Mont_Saint-Michel "Mont Saint-Michel").[[179]](#cite_note-MonkM-182)[[180]](#cite_note-CathMont-183)[[181]](#cite_note-pilgrim-184) In 960 the [Duke of Normandy](/wiki/Duke_of_Normandy "Duke of Normandy") commissioned a [Benedictine](/wiki/Order_of_St._Benedict "Order of St. Benedict") abbey on the mount, and it remains a major pilgrimage site.[[181]](#cite_note-pilgrim-184)

A [Portuguese](/wiki/Portugal "Portugal") [Carmelite](/wiki/Carmelites "Carmelites") nun, [Antónia d'Astónaco](/wiki/Ant%C3%B3nia_d%27Ast%C3%B3naco "Antónia d'Astónaco"), reported an apparition and [private revelation](/wiki/Private_revelation "Private revelation") of the [Archangel Michael](/wiki/Saint_Michael_(Roman_Catholic) "Saint Michael (Roman Catholic)") who had told to this devoted [Servant of God](/wiki/Servant_of_God#Roman_Catholicism "Servant of God"), in 1751, that he would like to be honored, and God glorified, by the praying of nine special invocations. These nine invocations correspond to invocations to the nine choirs of angels and origins the famous [Chaplet of Saint Michael](/wiki/Chaplet_of_Saint_Michael "Chaplet of Saint Michael"). This private revelation and prayers were approved by [Pope Pius IX](/wiki/Pope_Pius_IX "Pope Pius IX") in 1851.[[57]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBall2003123-59)[[182]](#cite_note-185)

From 1961 to 1965, four young schoolgirls had reported several apparitions of the Archangel Michael in the small village of [Garabandal](/wiki/San_Sebasti%C3%A1n_de_Garabandal "San Sebastián de Garabandal"), Spain. At Garabandal, the apparitions of the Archangel Michael were mainly reported as announcing the arrivals of the [Virgin Mary](/wiki/Blessed_Virgin_Mary "Blessed Virgin Mary"). The [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") has neither approved nor condemned the [Garabandal apparitions](/wiki/Garabandal_apparitions "Garabandal apparitions").[[183]](#cite_note-186)

## In literature, music, and art

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Michael_(archangel)&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: In literature, music, and art")]

### Literature

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Michael_(archangel)&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Literature")]

In the 1667 English [epic poem](/wiki/Epic_poem "Epic poem") *[Paradise Lost](/wiki/Paradise_Lost "Paradise Lost")* by [John Milton](/wiki/John_Milton "John Milton"), Michael commands the army of angels loyal to God against the rebel forces of [Satan](/wiki/Satan "Satan"). Armed with a sword from God's armory, he bests Satan in personal combat, wounding his side.[[184]](#cite_note-187)

In [Henry Wadsworth Longfellow](/wiki/Henry_Wadsworth_Longfellow "Henry Wadsworth Longfellow")'s translation of the mid-thirteenth century *[The Golden Legend](/wiki/The_Golden_Legend "The Golden Legend")*, Michael is one of the angels of the seven planets. He is the angel of Mercury.[[185]](#cite_note-188)

### Music

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Michael_(archangel)&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Music")]

[Marc-Antoine Charpentier](/wiki/Marc-Antoine_Charpentier "Marc-Antoine Charpentier"), *Praelium Michaelis Archangeli factum in coelo cum dracone*, H.410, oratorio for soloists, double chorus, strings and continuo (1683).[[186]](#cite_note-189)

### Artistic depictions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Michael_(archangel)&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Artistic depictions")]

Main article: [Archangel Michael in Christian art](/wiki/Archangel_Michael_in_Christian_art "Archangel Michael in Christian art")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/00/Meister_der_Ikone_des_Erzengels_Michael_001_adjusted.jpg/250px-Meister_der_Ikone_des_Erzengels_Michael_001_adjusted.jpg)](/wiki/File:Meister_der_Ikone_des_Erzengels_Michael_001_adjusted.jpg)

Tenth-century gold and enamel Byzantine [icon](/wiki/Icon "Icon") of [St Michael](/wiki/St_Michael "St Michael"), in the treasury of the St Mark's Basilica

In [Christian art](/wiki/Christian_art "Christian art"), Archangel Michael may be depicted alone or with other angels such as [Gabriel](/wiki/Gabriel "Gabriel"). Some depictions with Gabriel date back to the eighth century, e.g. the stone casket at [Notre Dame de Mortain](/wiki/Mortain "Mortain") church in France.[[187]](#cite_note-rich141-190)

The widely reproduced image of *[Our Mother of Perpetual Help](/wiki/Our_Mother_of_Perpetual_Help "Our Mother of Perpetual Help")*, an icon of the [Cretan school](/wiki/Cretan_school "Cretan school"), depicts Michael on the left carrying the lance and sponge of the [crucifixion of Jesus](/wiki/Crucifixion_of_Jesus "Crucifixion of Jesus"), with Gabriel on the right side of [Mary and Jesus](/wiki/Madonna_and_Child "Madonna and Child").[[188]](#cite_note-191)

In many depictions, Michael is represented as an angelic warrior, fully armed with helmet, sword, and shield.[[44]](#cite_note-Cathenc-46) The shield may bear the Latin inscription *[Quis ut Deus](/wiki/Quis_ut_Deus "Quis ut Deus")* or the Greek inscription *Christos Dikaios Krites* or its initials.[[189]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBall2003520-192) He may be standing over a serpent, a dragon, or the defeated figure of Satan, whom he sometimes pierces with a lance.[[44]](#cite_note-Cathenc-46) The iconography of Michael slaying a serpent goes back to the early fourth century, when [Emperor Constantine](/wiki/Constantine_the_Great "Constantine the Great") defeated [Licinius](/wiki/Licinius "Licinius") at the [Battle of Adrianople](/wiki/Battle_of_Adrianople_(324) "Battle of Adrianople (324)") in AD 324, not far from the *[Michaelion](/wiki/Michaelion "Michaelion")*, a church dedicated to Archangel Michael.[[42]](#cite_note-Richard-44)

Constantine felt that Licinius was an agent of Satan and associated him with the serpent described in the [Book of Revelation](/wiki/Book_of_Revelation "Book of Revelation") ([12:9](https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Bible_(American_Standard)/Revelation#12:9 "s:Bible (American Standard)/Revelation")).[[190]](#cite_note-193) After the victory, Constantine commissioned a depiction of himself and his sons slaying Licinius represented as a serpent a symbolism borrowed from the Christian teachings on the Archangel to whom he attributed the victory. A similar painting, this time with the Archangel Michael himself slaying a serpent, then became a major art piece at the Michaelion and eventually lead to the standard [iconography](/wiki/Iconography "Iconography") of the Archangel Michael as a [warrior saint](/wiki/Warrior_saint "Warrior saint").[[42]](#cite_note-Richard-44)

In less common depiction, Michael holds a pair of scales, weighs the souls of the departed and holds the [book of life](/wiki/Book_of_life "Book of life") (as in the [Book of Revelation](/wiki/Book_of_Revelation "Book of Revelation")) to show he partakes in the judgment.[[187]](#cite_note-rich141-190) [Michelangelo](/wiki/Michelangelo "Michelangelo") depicted this scene on the altar wall of the [Sistine Chapel](/wiki/Sistine_Chapel "Sistine Chapel").[[191]](#cite_note-194)

In [Byzantine art](/wiki/Byzantine_art "Byzantine art"), Michael was often shown as a princely court dignitary rather than a warrior who battled Satan or with scales for weighing souls on the [Day of Judgement](/wiki/Day_of_Judgement "Day of Judgement").[[192]](#cite_note-195)
